item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: SCP Foundation Wiki
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: 1613-new.png
Author: Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the title of a work; the comic is The Spoken Fool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is the title on the main list of skip's italicized? is that a mistake?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Both SCP-1613, and SCP-1854 are similar in that in each article depicts an artist using their craft to send a message against what they believe to be an evil, be that " the suffering of independent comic artists at the hands of large corporations" seen here, or an anti-consumerist statement as seen in 1854. There is a good chance that these kindred spirits may know each other, and therefore a link between similar articles can emerge. For those of you wondering "Why not AWCY then?" Roget specifically stated he wants to keep this particular article out of AWCY's hands. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1613 is believed to have originally been published in an independent magazine being distributed in the city of ███ ██████, in an independent newsletter distributed to local art groups. SCP-1613 did not display anomalous properties during this time. However, the original author, Mallory Locicero, left the publication shortly after she began working. Approximately 4 months later, SCP-1613 was anomalously published in the ███ █ Gazette, a small newspaper in ███ ██████. SCP-1613 was identified as an anomaly on 9/18/1977, and classified as Euclid.

To this:

SCP-1613 is believed to have originally been published in an independent magazine being distributed in the city of ███ ██████, in an independent newsletter distributed to local art groups. SCP-1613 did not display anomalous properties during this time. However, the original author, Mallory Locicero1, left the publication shortly after she began working. Approximately 4 months later, SCP-1613 was anomalously published in the ███ █ Gazette, a small newspaper in ███ ██████. SCP-1613 was identified as an anomaly on 9/18/1977, and classified as Euclid.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Investigation into Locicero by Foundation personnel has revealed loose connections to George Whitby and other known anartists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 - As someone who gets sucked into various art scenes and occasionally flips through journals and publications about mediums (like, for instance, comics) that get a lot of stereotypical shit and snobbery from established mediums (like, say, film or literature) and crawling over each other and decrying the mainstream to be more "artistic", the idea of someone trying to force a medium to be artsy-fartsy by forcibly chopping out everything "casual" amuses me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And somewhere Mallory sits and wonders … are we cool yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a coincidence; while I was Googling Pearls Before Swine because of something somebody said upthread, I found this. Last sentence second paragraph made me go 'huh'. So I link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that comic. Love it. Only comic that makes me laugh out loud.

"So he threw his lawyer off of a cliff"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can replicate the effect, but I could never recapture the emotions behind it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it looked more newspaper-y.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ksaid wouldn't have been able to duplicate it in the comment if they didn't already know how it's done ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, he has it telespaced (done with double braces) in addition to italics. 687 has the same thing going on. As for the reasons, I don't know. BUT THAT'S THE SCIENCE BEHIND THE ART.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yo, the title on the mainlist looks a bit dodgy. Most Title-titles are formatted like:

SCP-1613 - The Spoken Fool

But this one's like:

SCP-1613 - The Spoken Fool

What's up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does this thing get published at all? Do the people in charge of running the newspaper not realize that they are printing the same comic over and over when the takeover occurs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He was talking about Get Fuzzy you stooge

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe they hate being peers with that goddamn cat who hates Mondays.

Hey man, don't hate on Heathcliff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was wrong of me to comment briefly on this and not explain why this is so very, very good

This is not a SCP about an earnest artist who found himself disillusioned with the business of newspaper comics. Or rather, it is, but it is a much starker portrayal of how that plays out in reality. I still carry my fond memories of Calvin and Hobbes and a certain respect for Bill Watterson's work, but that strip was on the decline long before he pulled the plug. Thank god he quit while he was still ahead, or this is pretty much exactly what it would have turned into. A joyless rant from a sad little man who isn't content with just destroying his own work, he has to drag everyone else down with him.

I'm not sure what it is that makes some cartoonists break down like this, but they always blame everything else. The rules they agreed to play by the moment they put pencil to paper. Maybe it's the format they hate, maybe they hate their publishers who do all the work of making sure their shit see the light of day. Maybe they hate being peers with that goddamn cat who hates Mondays. But I see no bravery in their tantrums. It's like watching a middle aged father of two decide that he just never really loved this whole idea of holding a job and raising a family so he burns the house down with his wife and children locked inside.

Sorts' rules for cartooning: If you can't produce, don't. But never blame the system and never blame the audience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just stuck thinking of Buckley, which detracts from the SCP as a whole. I feel like i'd like this more without the relation. Meh. My fault here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, whatever. That's what I like about articles like this: everyone sees it differently, but ultimately everyone agrees that the piece as a whole is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a unique (in a good way) entry. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uh that's… that's not what I see at all but okay

I do agree this is good either way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it even could have overwritten some other comics. Calvin and Hobbes was a LOT less "corporate" than many other comics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is short and sweet, and on starting to read it I was quite sceptical as there are older self-propagating art forms on the site, but this has a melancholy to it, and the transcript is beautiful and the simplicity enables a good imagination to fill in the gaps, which as has been said above is half the fun of reading good articles. No hesitation on voting this one up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Calvin and Hobbes wasn't published until 1985. But your point still stands: it would suck if it got overwritten by SCP-1613.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My personal headcanon is that the comic gained some sort of sapience and somehow published itself after Mallory quit, and it now echoes Mallory's feelings of comic strips without her knowledge.

That being said, I absolutely love this for inspiring any sort of consideration beyond what's in the article. You do a great job at painting a picture while leaving out just enough to have us longing for more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The spaces they were formerly placed in will have text replaced with diatribes denouncing the comic author, the content, and the layout of the newspaper SCP-1613 is published in.

I'd love to see some examples of these. Not a flood of them, but some. Stuff like that is half the fun of pieces like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An anomalous newspaper comic is a neat idea, but I feel like there's something else going on here that I just don't get, and not getting it leaves the entire thing kind of "eh" for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one hits close to home for me in pretty much every way (as a Carolinean, an aspiring illustrator/comic writer, my opinion on most comics nowadays, a million other things) The only question I have is why does it skip NC, but hit SC and TN?

Either way, upvoted with extreme prejudice. (yay wordplay~)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this! Upboats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But Calvin and Hobbes were too strong for it! They didn't change.
Don't take them from me D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It'd probably be easier to shut the paper down then to convince them to permanently pull all comics forever.

e: I've also amended the containment procedures to address your other point

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the containment procedures are incomplete. What if this appeared in, say, the Washington Post? Plus, wouldn't it be easier to end the comics page than to shut down the whole newspaper?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if this thing was found in 1977, that would mean that this comic also tried to overwrite Peanuts and Calvin and Hobbes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Empty shells that existed in the hands of old, numb, and sometimes dead men.

I… can't find any counter-argument there. Except maybe Pearls before Swine.

Anyway. Upvoted with great justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, my; this is really wonderful. The art's lovely as well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. I especially love the exert from the final comic as it reminds me of someone reading a very bitter eulogy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I can easily see a tired artist, sick of shallow comics about fat cats and the trivial machinations of corporate puppets, drawing this to try and bring some intelligence to the masses.

Seriously, +1, and I think I've found another entry for my favorites list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one I've been working on since september, finally decided I'd buffed it enough to post on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it when I can try and piece together a backstory and the ideas and emotions behind the creator of an SCP. Upvoted without hesitation.

Note: read the containment procedures first.
